questions.explanation: With the subject I, the present simple uses the base verb with no -s: I play football. The -s ending ("plays") is only for he/she/it; "playing" and "to play" are not present-simple forms.

questions.prompt: Complete: "My brother ___ to school by bus."
questions.explanation: "My brother" = he, so add the third-person ending. The verb go ends in -o, so we add -es: He goes to school. "go" forgets the -s, "gos" is misspelled, and "going" is the -ing form.
With we (plural), use the base verb: We study English. The -ies spelling ("studies") belongs only to he/she/it, "studys" is never correct, and the last option is not a word.

She takes the third-person ending, and verbs ending in -ch add -es: She watches TV. "watch" forgets the ending, "watchs" is the wrong spelling (it must be -es after -ch), and "watching" is the -ing form.
They is plural, so use the base verb with no -s: They live in a small flat. "lives" is the he/she/it form, "living" is the -ing form, and "lifes" confuses the noun with the verb.

questions.prompt: Complete: "The baby ___ a lot at night."
questions.explanation: "The baby" = it, and cry ends in consonant + y, so y → i + es: The baby cries. "cry" forgets the ending, "crys" keeps the y wrongly, and "crying" is the -ing form.
